content.text: Custom Tools

Custom tools implement domain-specific logic that does not fit any standard category — financial calculations, text classification, address validation, report generation, or any business rule encoded as a function. They follow the same schema + handler pattern as API wrapper tools, but the handler contains your own logic rather than an HTTP call. Because they are pure functions with no external dependencies, they are fast, deterministic, and easy to test.

attrs.alt: Anatomy of a custom tool: schema the model sees, handler with your logic, and tests verifying the contract
attrs.title: Anatomy of a Custom Tool

content.text: Custom tools are where your domain expertise becomes accessible to the model. A well-described tool with precise parameter constraints is more reliable than any amount of prompt engineering about the same calculation.
Example: Financial Calculator

A return-on-investment calculator is a pure function — no I/O, no side effects. The schema defines the parameters precisely with types and constraints; the handler does the maths and returns a structured result.
Example: Text Classifier

A custom text classification tool encodes a business rule — ticket categorisation, sentiment analysis, content moderation — as a deterministic function rather than relying on the model to make the same classification inside its reasoning. This is faster, cheaper, and auditable.
Example: Address Validator

Address validation applies business rules before allowing an order to proceed. A custom tool encodes the rules once — format checks, known-invalid patterns, required fields — and the model calls it rather than trying to reason about address validity in natural language.
Composing Multiple Steps in One Tool

Sometimes a single tool should perform several related steps that always go together — fetching a price, applying a discount rule, and computing tax. Combining them avoids forcing the model into multiple round-trips for a single logical operation.
Custom Tools with Session State

Some custom tools need to track state across multiple calls in a session — a running total, a list of items accumulated, or a counter. Store session state in a class instance rather than module-level globals so it can be scoped per conversation.
Testing Custom Tools

Custom tool handlers are plain Python functions — they are easy to unit-test without mocking. Test the happy path, all validation error cases, and edge values like zero, negative numbers, and empty strings.

import pytest

def test_roi_positive():
    r = calculate_roi(investment=10000, revenue=15000, cost=8000)
    assert r["roi_percent"] == 70.0
    assert r["profit"] == 7000
    assert r["breakeven_revenue"] == 18000

def test_roi_zero_investment():
    r = calculate_roi(investment=0, revenue=5000, cost=1000)
    assert r["error"] == "VALIDATION_ERROR"
    assert r["retryable"] is False

def test_address_valid():
    r = validate_address("123 Main St", "San Francisco", "CA", "94105")
    assert r["valid"] is True
    assert r["normalised"]["state"] == "CA"

def test_address_bad_zip():
    r = validate_address("123 Main St", "Austin", "TX", "ABCDE")
    assert r["valid"] is False
    fields = [e["field"] for e in r["errors"]]
    assert "zip_code" in fields

def test_cart_accumulates():
    cart = ShoppingCartTools()
    cart.add_to_cart("Widget", 9.99, 2)
    cart.add_to_cart("Gadget", 24.99)
    view = cart.view_cart()
    assert view["count"] == 2
    assert view["subtotal"] == round(9.99 * 2 + 24.99, 2)
Summary
Custom tools encode domain-specific logic — calculations, classification, validation, report generation — as plain Python functions that are fast, deterministic, and unit-testable.
Follow the same schema + handler pattern as all other tools: schema defines what the model provides; handler implements the logic and returns a typed dict.
Return structured validation errors with the failing field name and constraint description so the model can correct its input rather than retry blindly.
Combine steps that always belong together (fetch, discount, tax) into one tool to avoid unnecessary round-trips.
Use a class instance for session-scoped state — add_to_cart, remove_from_cart, view_cart — rather than module-level globals that would leak across conversations.
Test every validation error path explicitly; custom tool handlers have no network dependencies, so tests run instantly and require no mocking.
Describe the tool's trigger condition clearly in the description: "Use this whenever the user asks about profitability" tells the model exactly when to call it.
